Jethro came to rejoice with Moses in the happiness of Israel, and to bring his wife and children to him. Moses needed to have his family with him, so that while he ruled the church of God, he could set a good example in family government.

Conversation about God's wonderful works is good, and it builds up faith. Jethro not only rejoiced in the honor done to his son-in-law, but in all the goodness done to Israel. Those who were watching were more affected by the favors God had shown to Israel than many of those who received them. Jethro gave glory to Israel's God. Whatever we have joy about, God must have praise. They joined in a sacrifice of thanksgiving. Mutual friendship is made holy by joint worship. It is very good for relatives and friends to join in the spiritual sacrifice of prayer and praise, as those who meet in Christ. This was a moderate feast; they ate bread, manna. Jethro needed to see and taste that bread from heaven, and though he was a gentile, he was welcome: gentiles are welcomed to Christ, the Bread of life.

Here is the great zeal and the toil of Moses as a magistrate. Having been employed to redeem Israel out of the house of bondage, he is also a type of Christ, because he is employed as a lawgiver and a judge among them. If the people were as quarrelsome with each other as they were with God, then no doubt Moses had many cases brought before him. This business Moses was called to; it appears that he did it with great care and kindness. The least Israelite was welcome to bring his case before him. Moses kept to his business from morning to night. Jethro thought it was too much for him to undertake alone, and also it would make the administration of justice tiresome to the people. There may be overdoing even in well-doing. Wisdom is profitable to direct us, so that we do not settle for less than our duty, nor burden ourselves beyond our strength. Jethro advised Moses about a better plan. Great men should not only study to be useful themselves, but also devise ways to make others useful. Care must be taken in the choice of the people admitted into such a trust. They should be men of good sense who understand business and are not discouraged by disapproval or complaints, but abhor the thought of a bribe. Men of piety and religion; those who fear God, who dare not do a dishonest thing, even if they could do it secretly and safely. The fear of God will best protect a man against temptations to injustice. Moses did not despise this advice. Those are not wise who think themselves too wise to be counselled.
